data.title: Versant Media agrees to sell SportsEngine to PlayMetrics - report
data.description:
Versant Media (VSNT), the owner of CNBC, Golf Channel and E!, agreed to sell the youth-sports app SportsEngine to PlayMetrics, a competitor that's backed by Genstar Capital. Versant shares rose 2.3%.

A deal could be announced as soon as Friday, according to a Bloomberg report, which cited people familiar with the matter. Terms of the transaction weren't immediately known.

Bloomberg News reported in October that Versant hired an advisor to review a sale that would value SportsEngine at $400 million to $500 million.

Versant and Genstar declined to comment to Bloomberg. A representative for PlayMetrics didn’t respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Pfizer, Arvinas breast cancer therapy approved by the FDA
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration on Friday approved vepdegestrant, a breast cancer therapy developed by Pfizer (PFE) and Arvinas (ARVN) under the brand name Veppanu.

Accordingly, the once-daily oral therapy will be available in the U.S. as a late-line option for adults with estrogen receptor-positive, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-negative, ESR1-mutated advanced or metastatic breast cancer.

With the approval, the FDA also authorized Guardant360 CDx, a companion diagnostic device developed by Guardant Health (GH) to identify breast cancer harboring ESR1 mutations.

In August, the FDA accepted a New Drug Application submitted by Arvinas (ARVN) for vepdegestran, assigning June 5, 2026, as the target action date.

The NDA was backed by data from the companies’ VERITAC-2 Phase 3 clinical trial, which indicated that patients on vepdegestran survived five months without cancer progression compared to 2.1 months for those who received fulvestrant, an established breast cancer drug.

data.title: AIG outlines low to mid-teens 2026 net premiums written growth as dividend rises 11% to $0.50
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American International Group (AIG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We had a very strong start to 2026 and delivered an exceptional first quarter, the strongest first quarter that we've seen since I've been at AIG," Quote (CEO & Chairman Peter Zaffino) and added that AIG is "delivering balanced net premiums written growth with excellent accident year combined ratios to support earnings expansion across our core businesses, while also focusing on our nominal expense base."

  • "General Insurance net premiums written increased 18% year-over-year on a constant dollar basis," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), including "Global Commercial Insurance" up "21%" and "Global Personal Insurance" up "11%."

  • "We grew net premiums written 11%" in Global Personal and "the accident year combined ratio as adjusted improved 570 basis points to 89.9%," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), while "the calendar year combined ratio was 89.4%, a strong improvement from 107.9% in the prior year."

  • "During the quarter, we returned $760 million of capital to shareholders," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), and "the AIG Board of Directors approved an 11% increase in our quarterly dividend to $0.50 per share starting in the second quarter of 2026."

  • "At the end of the first quarter, our equity interest in Corebridge was approximately 5.6%," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), adding, "We anticipate fully exiting our position by selling down our remaining stake in 2026, subject to market conditions" and "We expect the primary use of these proceeds will be for additional share repurchases."

  • "The Lexington large account shared and layered business in Excess & Surplus Lines... has been under significant pricing pressure," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), and "we have been contracting our Lexington large account portfolio, and you should expect that to continue throughout the year if the current market environment persists."

  • "This quarter, in close partnership with Palantir and Anthropic, we've begun the next phase of agentic AI at AIG," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), and said that in Lexington middle market property, AIG Assist "helped deliver a 30% improvement on quoting more submissions" and "reduced time to quote... by 55%."

  • "Adjusted pretax income was $1.5 billion," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Keith Walsh), and "Underwriting income more than tripled to $774 million year-over-year, driven by lower catastrophe losses, improved accident year underwriting results and higher favorable prior year reserve development."

  • "I want to reaffirm my commitment to the strategy and delivering on our Investor Day financial guidance," Quote (President Eric Andersen), including "operating EPS compound annual growth of over 20% over the 3 years ending 2027" and "core operating ROE of 10% to 13% through 2027."

Outlook

  • "For full year 2026, we continue to expect low to mid-teens net premium written growth in General Insurance," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh).

  • "Net premiums earned growth is expected to benefit AIG in the back half of 2026 and as we enter 2027," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino).

  • "We expect the second quarter Other Operations net investment income and other line to be in the range of $30 million to $40 million, subject to market conditions," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted after-tax income per diluted share was $2.11," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), and "Core operating ROE was 12.2%."

  • "On a constant dollar basis, General Insurance gross premiums written of $10 billion increased 7% year-over-year" and "Net premiums earned were $6.1 billion, up 5% year-over-year," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh).

  • "General Insurance accident year combined ratio as adjusted was 86.6%" and "the expense ratio of 29.3%," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh), with "Total catastrophe losses for the quarter... approximately $180 million."

  • "Prior year development... was $132 million favorable," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh), and "Overall, the General Insurance calendar year combined ratio was 87.3%."

  • "First quarter General Insurance net investment income was $864 million, up 17% year-over-year," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh), while "alternative investment income... was $6 million compared to $43 million in the prior year quarter."

Q&A

  • Meyer Shields, KBW: "as leading carriers and brokers, both successfully adopt AI, how does that impact what the carriers pay to the brokers?" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino): "I think scale will matter over time" and "the ability to collaborate will get even stronger."

  • Meyer Shields, KBW: "the impact of pricing on the Everest business" Quote (EVP & Chief Executive Officer of General Insurance Jon Hancock): "we're really pleased with this transaction" and "the retention and the conversion is really strong, the ratios actually are just as we expected."

  • Brian Meredith, UBS: "you're starting to see some cracks in Casualty" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino): "I do think the Casualty has become a little bit more under pressure from rates" but "it's not in the same bucket as the way the property has been performing."

  • Brian Meredith, UBS: "thoughts on deploying that excess capital" Quote (President Andersen): "I think the opportunity is in front of us today with the plan that we've laid out right now" and focused on "drive our organic growth" and "continue to execute on the transactions that have been done."

  • Jian Huang, Morgan Stanley: "there is a future state where your underwriting and your understanding of risk would be much more uniform globally" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino): "I absolutely think in a 5-year period that the global capabilities... across an organization... will be profound" while noting "Europe" and "GDPR" complexity.

  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: "should we just be -- just a little bit of kind of pressure from the soft market?" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino): "could there be some deterioration in the Property attritional loss ratios... There could be" and "we're highly confident that we can offset that with expense discipline, earned premium growth and the expense ratio will go down."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and congratulatory, with pointed, diligence-oriented follow-ups on market conditions and sustainability of margins, including "should we... not get too maybe complacent" Quote (Michael Zaremski, BMO).

  • Management tone was positive and confident in execution and discipline, including "we're highly confident" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), while also conceding competitive pressure in "Lexington large account" property and a watchful stance on casualty pricing.

  • Versus Q4 2025, the current call kept a similarly confident posture on strategy execution, but put more emphasis on near-term property pricing pressure and the operational detail of "agentic AI" deployment compared with prior-quarter focus on "gen AI" rollout and reinsurance renewal outcomes.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 messaging emphasized acceleration in written premium growth and underwriting performance, with "net premiums written increased 18%" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), compared with Q4 2025 where "Net premiums written were $6 billion, an increase of 1%," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh).

  • Property discussion sharpened in Q1 2026 around contraction in a specific E&S sub-portfolio, with "we have been contracting our Lexington large account portfolio," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), while Q4 2025 framed property as pressured but broadly managed, noting "Retail Property... contracted 8%" and "excess and surplus lines pricing was down 13% for the year," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino).

  • AI narrative moved from rollout and orchestration ambitions in Q4 2025 ("we're exploring the next phase... focused on the orchestration of AI agents" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino)) to Q1 2026 describing the start of "the next phase of agentic AI" with "Palantir and Anthropic," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino).

  • Leadership transition progressed from announcement to timing: Q4 2025 said Eric Andersen would join as "President and CEO-elect" Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), while Q1 2026 confirmed he "will officially become our next CEO on June 1," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino).

Risks and Concerns

  • "The direct impact on AIG is not material based on what we've seen to date, but we're not complacent," Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino), citing actions including "monitoring accumulation risk, adjusting underwriting guidelines where warranted, stress testing our investment portfolio and staying very close to our reinsurance partners."

  • "The U.S. Property market environment remains very competitive," Quote (President Andersen), while management highlighted portfolio actions including being "more selective on new business" and "willing to non-renew accounts" that no longer meet expected returns, Quote (CEO & Chairman Zaffino).

  • "We expect second quarter alternative returns to remain below our expectations," Quote (Executive VP & CFO Walsh), and said AIG has "slowed our deployment" in private credit "given market conditions."

Final Takeaway

AIG management described Q1 2026 as its strongest first quarter under Peter Zaffino, driven by 18% constant-dollar General Insurance net premiums written growth, improved underwriting ratios, and higher profitability, while highlighting a more selective posture in pressured U.S. large-account E&S property. The company reiterated expectations for low to mid-teens 2026 General Insurance net premiums written growth, increased the quarterly dividend 11% to $0.50 starting Q2 2026, and reiterated plans to fully exit Corebridge in 2026 with proceeds primarily intended for additional share repurchases, alongside a continued push into “agentic AI” partnerships with Palantir and Anthropic to improve underwriting and claims productivity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aon reaffirms 70 to 80 bps 2026 margin expansion as it targets at least $1B in share repurchases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Aon plc (AON) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We started 2026, the final year of our 3x3 Plan, with strong momentum," said Gregory Case (President, CEO & Executive Director), adding that Aon is "reaffirming our guidance for 2026" and that management "remain[s] confident in our long-term outlook."

  • Case highlighted Aon Business Services (ABS) and AI as central enablers of the strategy, saying, "By year-end, we expect to have invested approximately $1.3 billion in talent and technology," and arguing these investments are "materially improving productivity and execution for clients."

  • Case pointed to data centers as a growth vector tied to AI-driven demand, stating, "Our data center life cycle insurance program, which we recently increased capacity by another $1 billion to $3.5 billion, allows our firm to lead as a market maker."

  • Edmund Reese (Executive VP & Chief Financial Officer) tied the quarter to the company’s financial model, saying, "The fundamentals of our business are strong, resilient and evident in our results," and reiterating the company’s organic growth objective as "mid-single-digit or greater."

  • Reese described opportunistic capital deployment in the quarter, stating, "[W]e recognized the unique market conditions and opportunistically deployed $500 million to repurchase shares," and also noted Aon "allocated $349 million toward high-growth tuck-in acquisitions in middle market."

Outlook

  • Reese reaffirmed 2026 guidance, stating, "[W]e are reaffirming our 2026 full year guidance for mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth" and "reaffirming our expectations for 70 to 80 basis points of margin expansion for the full year."

  • Reese reiterated capital return intentions, saying Aon "remain[s] firmly on track to deliver at least $1 billion in share repurchases for the year" while continuing to pursue M&A under stated return thresholds.

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s framework for 2026 guidance was consistent; in Q4 2025 Reese said, "our full year '26 guidance includes mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, 70 to 80 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion," and in Q1 2026 he said Aon is "reaffirming" those same elements.

Financial Results

  • Reese reported, "total revenue increased 6% year-over-year to $5 billion" and "Adjusted EPS was up 14% to $6.48."

  • Reese said profitability expanded, reporting, "Adjusted operating margin expanded by 70 basis points and reached 39.1%," and added, "Q1 adjusted operating income was up 8% to $2 billion."

  • Reese reported cash generation, stating, "we generated $363 million in free cash flow," and reiterated that Aon expects "double-digit free cash flow growth in 2026."

  • On segment performance, Reese said, "In Commercial Risk, organic revenue growth of 7% marked the fourth consecutive quarter of growth at 6% or higher," while also citing construction as a driver and noting, "our data center revenue pipeline is on pace to be 3x higher than last year."

  • Reese flagged pricing pressure in Reinsurance renewals, stating, "Looking ahead to the second quarter, our data points to further rate pressure at April 1 renewals with rates down 15% to 20% in both the U.S. and Japan," while also saying the company continues to expect full-year organic growth in line with objectives.

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC: Asked for sizing of data center contribution to Q1 organic growth and expectations; Edmund Reese (Executive VP & Chief Financial Officer) said data centers were "a part of the double-digit construction" growth but "it wasn't the key driver of growth," and Gregory Case (President, CEO & Executive Director) added Aon is "at the beginning of the beginning with tremendous promise ahead."

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC: Asked why the company did not raise its buyback target after Q1 repurchases; Reese emphasized "our capital allocation model," cited leverage at "2.7" versus an objective "at least 2.9," and said, "for now, I think it is prudent for us to stick with our at least $1 billion in the year."

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies LLC: Asked about new logos vs. expanded mandates (especially Commercial Risk); Reese said recent history has been "split about half and half" and that Commercial Risk new business contribution reflected "an equal mix between the new logos and expanding."

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies LLC: Asked about commissions and fee levels; Case said, "the ultimate arbiter of truth here is clients," and positioned the environment as "real opportunity" tied to rising client needs and AI as "a catalyst."

  • Robert Cox, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: Asked how the Middle East conflict showed up in results and potential for claims inflation; Reese said the region had "double-digit growth" but is "not a substantial part of our overall business," and added, "if we continue to see an escalation or a prolonged conflict, that could have some impact" economically.

  • Michael Zaremski, BMO Capital Markets: Asked if Q2 property season could be a near-term governor on net market impact; Reese reiterated net market impact guidance of "0 to 2" points and said the company expects performance "throughout the rest of the year, including Q2." Case responded, "We've taken in a very measured, methodical approach."

  • Jian Huang, Morgan Stanley: Asked about AI-related expenses and whether variable costs are in guidance; Reese replied, "yes. It is factored into the margin guidance," and said costs are "baked into the $1.3 billion investment" plan.

  • Cave Montazeri, Deutsche Bank AG: Asked whether ABS advantages risk commoditization as peers invest; Case argued the company’s edge comes from data, analytics, and "organization" changes, adding, "we're just going to keep investing in that engine and driving that engine."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on sustainability of Commercial Risk growth into a key quarter, conflict spillover risk, and AI cost visibility; examples included Zaremski’s "shouldn't get over our skis" framing and Huang’s focus on variable, usage-driven AI expense.

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and consistent in Q&A, repeatedly using reaffirmation language; Reese said, "The fundamentals of our business are strong" and "we are reaffirming" guidance, while also providing guardrails on M&A thresholds and cost control.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s confidence language remained elevated; in Q4 2025 Case said, "Our conviction and level of excitement ... has never been greater," while Q1 2026 emphasized execution continuity and that Aon is "reaffirming" guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic emphasis stayed centered on the 3x3 Plan, ABS, and AI, but Q1 2026 added more quantified operational productivity examples and updated data center capacity to "$3.5 billion" from Q4 2025’s stated "$2.5 billion" capacity.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4 2025’s heavier emphasis on available capital and M&A deployment to Q1 2026 scrutiny on near-term drivers: data center contribution sizing, Q2 pricing pressure in reinsurance, and AI cost scalability.

  • Guidance posture was stable: Q4 2025 introduced 2026 targets; Q1 2026 repeatedly stated it was "reaffirming" them.

Risks and Concerns

  • Reese highlighted pricing pressure in reinsurance renewals, saying April 1 renewals show "rates down 15% to 20%" in the U.S. and Japan, and positioned mitigation through demand, new business activity, and mix.

  • Management addressed geopolitical uncertainty, with Case noting "ongoing conflict in the Middle East" and Reese saying escalation "could have some impact" on broader economic conditions.

  • Reese cited slower demand in parts of Human Capital, stating Health Solutions was "partially offset by slower discretionary spend in Talent Solutions," and positioned offset via "increasing" demand for analytics and advisory.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a continuation of the 3x3 Plan playbook, combining mid-single-digit or greater organic growth expectations with 70 to 80 basis points of margin expansion guidance, while framing ABS and AI-embedded tools as drivers of both client value and operating leverage. They highlighted data centers as an expanding opportunity—alongside broader Commercial Risk momentum—while keeping capital allocation centered on disciplined tuck-in M&A and at least $1 billion of 2026 share repurchases, with Q1 including a $500 million accelerated buyback under what the CFO called opportunistic deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Recession odds drop below 20% amid U.S.-Iran peace hopes
data.description:
Traders on prediction markets are scaling back bets on a U.S. recession this year as optimism grows that the Iran conflict may be nearing an end. That's on top of strong corporate earnings.

Kalshi’s recession market showed an 18.2% chance Friday, dropping to below the 20% threshold after weeks of elevated readings driven in large part by high energy (USO) (BNO) (UNG) prices from the war.

The move comes as the stock market (SPY) (VOO) advanced to a new intraday record high as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note, with oil prices retreating after Iran reportedly submitted its response via Pakistani mediators to the latest U.S. amendments to a draft deal to end the Middle East war.

In addition, earnings season kept going strong as Apple (AAPL) turned in better-than-expected results for fiscal Q2, as the iPhone 17 line continued to impress consumers.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: LRHC tops REIT bear's list under $2B market cap; SACH least shorted in April
data.description:
The State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) closed at 44.39, down -0.02% on the day. The ETF has shown a 10.01% price return YTD, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which has a YTD price return of 5.31%.

XLRE carries a risk grade of D+ with a short interest of 3.48% as a percentage of shares outstanding. 

Short interest in micro-cap ($300M) and small-cap ($300M-$2B) real estate and mortgage REITs shows notable divergence, with some stocks seeing high SI while others have relatively low levels.

Here’s a breakdown of the most and least shorted stocks in the real estate and mortgage REIT sectors:

Top 5 most shorted real estate and mortgage REITs as of April:

  • La Rosa Holdings (LRHC); Short Interest: 48.18%
  • Arbor Realty Trust (ABR); Short Interest: 23.79%
  • New Concept Energy (GBR); Short Interest: 19.44%
  • JBG SMITH Properties (JBGS); Short Interest: 16.64%
  • Presidio Property Trust (SQFT); Short Interest: 15.05%

Top 5 least shorted real estate and mortgage REITs as of April:

  • Sachem Capital (SACH); Short Interest: 0.53%
  • Granite Point Mortgage Trust (GPMT); Short Interest: 0.53%
  • Strawberry Fields (STRW); Short Interest: 0.55%
  • Ohmyhome (OMH); Short Interest: 0.57%
  • Five Point (FPH); Short Interest: 0.59%

This week, mortgage rates have risen following three consecutive weeks of decline. According to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey, 30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.30% as of April 30, up from 6.23% last week but still lower than the year-ago level of 6.76%, reported an SA analyst.

This slight rise could impact investor sentiment and market positioning in both sectors. As for the analyst sentiment, XLRE has a sell rating from SA analysts with an average rating of 2.00, while the quantitative rating suggests a hold with a 2.69.

Here are some other notable XLRE REIT ETFs that investors may want to track: iShares Select US REIT ETF (ICF), iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR), iShares Core US REIT ETF (USRT), Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), iShares Global REIT ETF (REET), iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (REM), Vanguard Global ex-US Real Estate Index Fd ETF (VNQI), and iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: Fulgent outlines $350M 2026 revenue outlook following Bako/StrataDx acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fulgent Genetics (FLGT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “I am pleased with our first quarter results in our laboratory service business and the momentum in our therapeutic development business,” said Chairman & CEO Ming Hsieh, adding, “in Q1, we also successfully completed the acquisition of Bako Diagnostics and StrataDx, which contributed our strong first quarter results as we had anticipated.”

  • On digital pathology, Chairman & CEO Ming Hsieh said, “the investments in AI and digital pathology solutions are continuing to work at an accelerated pace,” and added, “as of today, with our in-house developed platform, EasioPath, we are approximately 100% visual across all our cases.”

  • On the therapeutic pipeline, Chairman & CEO Ming Hsieh said “FID-007, advanced through Phase II with 46 patients enrolled,” and added, “our abstract on the Phase III trial of FID-007 was selected by ASCO as a rapid oral presentation.” He also said, “we anticipate having end of Phase II meeting with FDA for the second half of this year and hope to enter into a Phase III registration trial…in the first half of 2027.”

  • Chief Commercial Officer Brandon Perthuis reported, “we ended the first quarter at $71.1 million,” and said the decline was “driven by the reduction in sales to our large customer who has begun transitioning testing in-house.” He also said the Bako/StrataDx deal “more than doubles the size of our pathology sales team,” and added, “the focus now shifts to integration, which is off to a very good start.”

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said, “revenue in the first quarter of 2026 totaled $71.1 million, including $2.6 million from Bako Diagnostics and StrataDx,” and attributed the decline primarily to “lower volume from our largest customer… and timing impact as we work through claims processing backlog.”

Outlook

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said, “we reiterate our guidance of total revenue of $350 million for 2026,” and added it represents “an 8.5% year-over-year growth.”

  • On segment expectations, Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said, “we continue to estimate Precision Diagnostics revenues to be approximately $168 million, Anatomic Pathology to be approximately $162 million and Biopharma Services to be approximately $20 million.”

  • On profitability trajectory, Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said, “we expect non-GAAP gross margins for the full year to be approximately 39%,” and added, “we anticipate the gross margins to improve in the second quarter… and then to further improve to approximately 42% by the end of the year,” while “non-GAAP operating margin” is expected to be “a minus 20% for the year.”

  • On share count and EPS framing, Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said, “our forecasted average fully diluted share count for 2026 has decreased from 32 million shares to approximately 29 million shares,” and added, “we expect our full year 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance to decrease by $0.14 to a loss of $1.59 per share.”

Financial Results

  • Q1 revenue was $71.1M vs. $68.09298M (analysts’ estimate).

  • Non-GAAP loss per share was (-$0.36) vs. (-$0.35) (analysts’ estimate).

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said GAAP gross margin was “30.2%” and non-GAAP gross margin was “32.3%,” adding, “we expect gross margins to normalize as the backlog clears in the coming quarters and as revenue increases.”

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said GAAP loss was “$24.8 million… and a GAAP loss of $0.08 per share,” and added, “adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter was a loss of approximately $15.2 million.”

  • On liquidity and near-term cash items, Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said the company ended Q1 with “approximately $604.7 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and marketable securities,” and added, “we have not yet received $106 million federal income tax refund.”

Q&A

  • Lu Li, UBS Investment Bank: “If you're excluding the largest customer impact, what is the underlying business growth…?” Paul Kim (CFO): “if you strip that away… we do have growth in the precision diagnostics area for this year.”

  • Lu Li, UBS Investment Bank: “Any reasons why [gross margin is] a little bit lower… and… how comfortable… to… get back to… 40% in the second half?” Paul Kim (CFO): “the lower gross margins are coming from the lower-than-anticipated revenues,” driven by “timing impact from claims delayed,” and Brandon Perthuis (CCO) added, “Q1 historically has been a little bit softer for us… we did have our laboratories shut down multiple times due to weather.”

  • Lu Li, UBS Investment Bank: “any… impacts” from “CMS CRUSH initiative?” Brandon Perthuis (CCO): “not at this time, Lu. We don't have any comment on that.”

  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: “What was… the contribution from StrataDx and Bako… and… what should Q2 look like?” Paul Kim (CFO): “it was $2.6 million,” and added, “Q2 should be a higher quarter… we also get the full quarter of Bako and StrataDx,” with “targets… in excess of $90 million per quarter in terms of revenues” for Q2–Q4.

  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: What drives Precision Diagnostics growth? Brandon Perthuis (CCO): “we benefit tremendously from our diverse portfolio of tests,” citing momentum in “exomes and genomes,” “a rapid and ultra-rapid genome… as quick as 48 hours,” continued performance from “Beacon,” and “very recent momentum in pharmacogenetic testing related to this DPYD gene.”

  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: Biopharma visibility/book-to-bill? Paul Kim (CFO): “we continue to see lumpiness in our biopharma business… large transactions with long sales cycles.”

  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: FID-007 commercialization/partnering? Ming Hsieh (CEO): “we do have the options for the collaborations with potential partners,” while adding, “we want to present the opportunity when we do the collaboration at a strength, not at a weakness.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly negative, focusing on customer concentration, margins, reimbursement/regulatory items, and visibility, including “how comfortable… to kind of like get back… to kind of like 40%” (Lu Li, UBS Investment Bank) and “what should Q2 look like?” (David Westenberg, Piper Sandler).

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing normalization and execution, including “we expect gross margins to normalize” (Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim) and “we are confident in the strength of our team” (Chief Commercial Officer Brandon Perthuis).

  • Versus last quarter, management maintained an upbeat posture around AI/digital pathology and pipeline progress, while Q&A in Q1 leaned more into short-term revenue timing and margin pressure tied to “claims processing backlog” (Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 highlighted completion of Bako Diagnostics and StrataDx, versus Q4 when management said it “announced our intention to acquire… pending regulatory approvals for a total purchase price of $55.5 million” (Chief Commercial Officer Brandon Perthuis, prior call).

  • Q1 results reflected a larger sequential revenue step-down alongside “lower volume from our largest customer” and a “claims processing backlog” (Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim), whereas Q4 framed early impacts primarily as “lower-than-anticipated volume from our largest customer” (Chief Commercial Officer Brandon Perthuis, prior call).

  • Guidance language was reiterated at $350M revenue, while EPS and cash outlook language shifted to incorporate buybacks; in Q1, Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said EPS guidance changes were “solely attributable to decreased shares resulting from the stock repurchase program.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim cited continued near-term pressure from the largest customer transition: “we expect revenue from this customer in the second quarter to continue to be impacted by a significant decrease in volume,” with potential stabilization “in the second half of the year.”

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim flagged operational timing: “timing impact as we work through claims processing backlog,” while also stating, “we expect gross margins to normalize as the backlog clears.”

  • Chief Financial Officer Paul Kim said the company has “not yet received $106 million federal income tax refund,” describing delays tied to “constrained resources at the IRS.”

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 around integrating Bako/StrataDx to expand anatomic pathology scale, navigating near-term revenue and margin headwinds tied to the largest customer transition and claims processing backlog, and progressing two oncology assets with FID-007 moving toward an FDA end-of-Phase II meeting in the second half of 2026 and a targeted Phase III registration trial start in the first half of 2027, while reiterating 2026 revenue guidance of $350 million and updating non-GAAP EPS and cash expectations to reflect stock repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BP considering sale of U.K. North Sea assets - Bloomberg
data.description:
BP (BP) is considering a potential exit of all or part of its operations in the U.K. North Sea, Bloomberg reported Friday, as it works to dispose of assets and pay down debt.

The company is conducting an internal review of its upstream operations in the U.K., which could fetch ~£2B ($2.7B) in a full divestment, according to the report.

BP (BP) is among the last remaining oil majors operating in the U.K. North Sea, as peers such as Chevron and ConocoPhillips sold assets, while others including Shell, Exxon, and TotalEnergies have moved to divest or restructure parts of their positions.

BP (BP) still holds a 45% stake in the Clair Field—the largest oil field on the U.K. Continental Shelf—but the company has been reducing its presence in the region for years, including sales of its interest in the Shearwater field to Shell and the Forties pipeline system to Ineos.

data.title: Matthews targets at least $180m adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2026 while planning a Propelis exit in 12-18 months
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Matthews International Corporation (MATW) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "On our last earnings call, we said that we were focused on execution, and we did just that in the second quarter," said "The redemption of our high-cost notes is complete. Our balance sheet is significantly improved. Interest expense is down materially." (CEO, President & Director Joseph Bartolacci)

  • "In January, we completed the early redemption of our $300 million of senior secured notes," and "Our total long-term debt is now $579 million, down from $822 million 1 year ago," while "Net debt stands at approximately $543 million today." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Propelis is continuing to perform well above the $100 million EBITDA run rate that was assumed when we structured the transaction," and "their EBITDA run rate is expected to be around $130 million going into 2027," with "an exit from this investment within the next 12 to 18 months." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "We shipped our first production units to paying customers," and "the commercial response to Acxiom remains strong," while management "expanded our total addressable market estimate to about $3 billion" and said it "continue[s] to actively pursue and engage in strategic partnership discussions, including white label opportunities." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "For the fiscal 2026 second quarter, the company reported a net loss of $21.8 million or $0.69 per share," and "On a non-GAAP adjusted basis, net income attributable to the company for the current quarter was $11.6 million or $0.37 per share." (CFO & Treasurer Daniel Stopar)

Outlook

  • "With regard to our full year outlook, we set guidance of at least $180 million in adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026, inclusive of our 40% interest in Propelis," and "we are reaffirming our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance of $180 million." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Achieving the full year target requires a stronger second half," with drivers cited as "Memorialization continuing its current trajectory, Industrial Technologies converting its pipeline and Propelis continuous operational execution." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Several things may impact that forecast," including "The pace and timing of engineering orders, the outcome of current tariff discussions at the federal level, the timing of synergies at Propelis us and the economic impact of geopolitical challenges." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Analysts’ EPS and revenue estimates were provided, but the JSON structure did not include a valid quarter format (Q1-Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial results

  • "Total revenues were $259 million compared to $428 million a year ago," and "Adjusted EBITDA for the fiscal 2026 second quarter was $45 million compared to $51 million in the prior year second quarter." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the fiscal 2026 second quarter was $44.7 million compared to $51.4 million a year ago," and "The decline reflected lower operating performance by the Engineering business within the Industrial Technologies segment." (CFO & Treasurer Stopar)

  • "Our Cornerstone segment reported sales of $215 million for the second quarter," with "adjusted EBITDA of $49 million," and "The Dodge acquisition continues to contribute meaningfully, adding approximately $10 million in sales per quarter and is ahead of our EBITDA targets." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • "Cash flow used in operating activities for the 6 months ended March 31, 2026, was $67.4 million," and management cited cash uses including "income taxes, transaction fees and repayments of securitized receivables" and that "Expenditures for litigation and proxy defense also consumed significant cash." (CFO & Treasurer Stopar)

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about organic Memorialization growth beyond Dodge and whether Matthews is seeing more M&A inbound; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci replied, "our volume [is] stable to modestly down" and said M&A interest exists but "I wouldn't say there's a lot of inbounds, but there are opportunities out there. We'll pick timing based on when it's right for us."

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked for more detail on Propelis SAP execution timing; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "we are at the middle" and "they will start that migration in about 90 days," adding, "we're very confident on their ability to execute going forward."

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about next steps after the Tesla arbitration decision and whether customer engagement improved; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "it's given a lot of clarity" and "they have opened more doors in the last 60 days or so," adding, "We have expanded our geographies to include Japan" and "we are emboldened by it."

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about how quickly ultracapacitor opportunity could come and breadth of customer participation; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "We have the 3 largest producers of ultracapacitors at our doorstep today" and described "multiple forms of partnership" plus a Germany-based production system "being commissioned as we speak."

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about reshoring-driven battery manufacturing demand and military-linked supply chain requirements; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "We're having significant discussions with solid-state manufacturers" and referenced "a relatively large order for North America battery separators" expected "over the course of the next 3 months, 4 months or so."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked to confirm Dodge revenue contribution and divestiture impacts; CFO & Treasurer Stopar replied, "$166 million from the divestitures" and confirmed the "$11 million contribution" from Dodge referenced in prepared remarks.

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked why Propelis equity-method figures appeared below a $100 million-plus run rate; CFO & Treasurer Stopar said, "Their slowest quarter is typically the fourth calendar quarter."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked whether Memorialization beat expectations and about inflation/average-cost inventory timing; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci said, "the quarter actually performed better at an execution level, worse at a revenue level," adding, "Price is consistent with what we had expected and -- but execution was even better."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked about tariffs embedded in forecasts and whether headwinds increased; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci replied, "Modest, yes," and said, "There's an expectation in our forecast for some impact of that."

  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC: Asked for key one-time cash cost buckets and strategic review costs; CFO & Treasurer Stopar cited "tax payments," "deal fees" and settling "securitized receivables," and later said, "Yes, we're about $55 million" on securitized receivables; CEO, President & Director Bartolacci added, "there's no ongoing costs associated with that" strategic review.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on cash usage, tariff exposure, Propelis timing/seasonality, and the practical impact of the Tesla ruling (e.g., "what are the next steps" and "are there examples... regarding engagement with new potential customers").

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A on legal/IP and execution, including "this ruling removes a key overhang" and "we are emboldened by it," while maintaining caution on near-term contributors ("Action will not be a material contributor to the top line this year"). (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from announcing leverage progress and divestiture execution to emphasizing post-redemption flexibility and legal clarity around DBE, while keeping the same full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance level. (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q2, management emphasized completion and financial impact of the note redemption ("interest expense is down materially" and "reducing annual interest expense by approximately $10 million"), while Q1 framed the redemption as expected to "reduce our annual interest expense by $12 million." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Product Identification messaging progressed from Q1’s paused shipments for refinements to Q2 commercialization, with management reporting, "we shipped our first production units to paying customers" after resolving beta issues. (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • The Propelis timeline tightened in language from Q1’s "18- to 24-month window" to Q2’s "12 to 18 months" for an expected exit, alongside a new point that Matthews "expect[s] to receive a partial redemption of our preferred interest in the coming quarter." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Analysts’ Q2 questions concentrated on Propelis SAP execution, tariff assumptions, and Tesla-arbitration implications, while Q1 questions leaned more toward balance sheet optimization and energy pipeline cadence. (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.; Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer; Justin Bergner, Gabelli Funds, LLC)

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited variables that could affect the full-year outlook: "The pace and timing of engineering orders," "the outcome of current tariff discussions," "the timing of synergies at Propelis," and "the economic impact of geopolitical challenges." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Industrial Technologies remained flagged as the swing factor, with management noting the segment "remains challenged" and planning "additional cost reduction actions within the engineering business in the second half to protect cash." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

  • Cash flow pressure in the first half was attributed to discrete items and seasonality, with management stating, "We expect both Q3 and Q4 to generate positive operating cash flow." (CEO, President & Director Bartolacci)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as execution on balance-sheet repair and a return to clearer second-half planning, anchored by reaffirmed fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $180 million. Leadership highlighted Memorialization’s continued EBITDA growth and Dodge integration progress, while positioning Propelis as a near-term cash catalyst (preferred redemption) and a targeted exit within 12 to 18 months. Industrial Technologies was characterized as the key variable, with first commercial shipments in Product Identification, a growing engineering pipeline, continued partnership discussions, and a favorable interim arbitration outcome on DBE that management said has reduced customer hesitation and expanded engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paramount Skydance, Warner Bros. merger a 'big, bold, and game-changing move'—Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley delivered a double upgrade to Paramount Skydance (PSKY) as the proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) presents a “big, bold, and game-changing move” that positions the company with some of the best intellectual properties in the world, including Game of Thrones, Harry Potter, and Lord of the Rings.

The firm now rates the entertainment conglomerate as Overweight from a prior Underweight rating and lifts the target price to $14, representing 37% upside to Thursday’s close.

While analyst Sean Diffley considers the double upgrade the firm’s “riskiest and most out-of-consensus call,” he considers three underappreciated elements to the PSKY/WBD merger, including the potential for AI to unlock significant cost savings and increase ad revenue, confidence in achieving $6B of synergies, and a clear mix shift to faster-growing streaming and studio assets.

Diffley acknowledges that as there are regulatory hurdles to the deal being consummated, his upgrade is contingent on approval. However, arguments in favor of regulatory approval include the lack of dominant market share in any vertical and a compelling case that the combined company would be pro-consumer, pro-creative talent, and pro-competition.

If, however, the deal doesn’t go through, a standalone Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is much less compelling than a combined company.

The upgrade breathed life into Paramount Skydance (PSKY) shares, snapping a six-day losing streak with a gain of more than 8%.
